verdict_reasoning: ATP5F1B (ATP synthase F1 subunit beta) is canonically the beta subunit of the mitochondrial F1Fo-ATP synthase, residing in the mitochondrial inner membrane matrix-facing F1 head. It has no TM domain, GPI anchor, or signal peptide for the classical secretory pathway. However, substantial published evidence documents ecto-ATP synthase: a pool of ATP synthase complex — including the beta subunit — is present on the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane in multiple cell types (endothelial cells, hepatocytes, adipocytes, tumor cells). On endothelial cells, ecto-ATP5F1B serves as a receptor for apoA-I and angiostatin. In cancer cells, surface ATP synthase (including beta subunit) is well-documented and is a proposed therapeutic target. This constitutes a documented cancer/cell-state-induced and cell-type-restricted surface pool qualifying as contextual/cell_state_induced. The mechanism of PM translocation is not fully understood (no classical secretory pathway), but the ecto-pool is wash-resistant and functionally active. No narrow germline restriction; no lysosomal exocytosis story; no TM-partner covalent tethering documented.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Mechanism of mitochondrial-to-PM translocation is not fully resolved; it is unclear whether the surface pool is truly wash-resistant or whether expression in non-cancer, non-endothelial baseline cells is sufficient to call it broadly contextual vs. tissue-restricted.
